docs.title: AerisPlane
docs.text:
Conceptual MDO toolkit for fixed-wing RC/UAV aircraft design (1–20 kg class).

AerisPlane is a Python library for analysing and optimising small unmanned aircraft at the conceptual design stage. It covers aerodynamics, weights, structures, stability, control authority, and mission performance — all wired together through a lightweight MDO layer.

docs.title: Module overview
docs.text: Module Description aerisplane.core Geometry dataclasses: Aircraft, Wing, Fuselage, FlightCondition, ... aerisplane.aero Aerodynamic analysis (VLM, LiftingLine, AeroBuildup, flow viz) aerisplane.weights Component mass buildup, CG analysis aerisplane.structures Euler–Bernoulli wing beam solver aerisplane.stability Numerical stability derivatives aerisplane.control Control authority and servo loads aerisplane.mission Point-performance energy budget aerisplane.propulsion Motor/battery/propeller operating point aerisplane.mdo Optimisation: design variables, constraints, SciPy/pygmo drivers aerisplane.catalog Hardware database — motors, batteries, propellers, servos

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
import aerisplane as ap
from aerisplane.aero import analyze

wing = ap.Wing(
    name="main_wing",
    symmetric=True,
    xsecs=[
        ap.WingXSec(xyz_le=[0.00, 0.00, 0.00], chord=0.28, airfoil=ap.Airfoil(name="ag35")),
        ap.WingXSec(xyz_le=[0.07, 1.20, 0.05], chord=0.14, twist=-2.0, airfoil=ap.Airfoil(name="ag35")),
    ],
)
aircraft = ap.Aircraft(name="MyPlane", wings=[wing])
cond = ap.FlightCondition(velocity=16.0, altitude=0.0, alpha=4.0)
result = analyze(aircraft, cond, method="vlm")
result.report()

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text: All notable changes to AerisPlane are documented here. Format follows Keep a Changelog.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
Aerodynamics (aerisplane.aero) - Vortex Lattice Method (VLM) — inviscid 3-D, arbitrary geometry - Prandtl Lifting Line with NeuralFoil section polars - Nonlinear Lifting Line (fixed-point stall iteration) - AeroBuildup semi-empirical method (NeuralFoil wings + Jorgensen fuselage) - Surface pressure and streamline flow visualisation - Interactive Plotly 3-D flow field (plot_interactive())

Weights (aerisplane.weights) - Component-based mass buildup from geometry and material properties - CG computation and inertia tensor estimate - Override mechanism for replacing estimates with measurements

Structures (aerisplane.structures) - Euler–Bernoulli wing beam solver - Failure checks: bending margin, shear margin, buckling margin - Tip deflection ratio and torsional divergence speed - OpenAeroStruct adapter (optional dependency)

Stability (aerisplane.stability) - Numerical stability derivatives via central finite differences - Static margin, neutral point, Cm_alpha, CL_alpha - Lateral-directional: Cl_beta, Cn_beta - Rate derivatives: CL_q, Cm_q, Cl_p, Cn_p, CY_p, Cn_r, Cl_r, CY_r - Dynamic mode estimates: short-period frequency and damping

Control (aerisplane.control) - Control authority: roll rate, pitch authority, rudder authority - Servo hinge moment estimation and servo load margin - Finite-difference control derivatives

Mission (aerisplane.mission) - Segment-based energy budget: Climb, Cruise, Loiter, Descent, Return - Flight envelope: Vs, Vmin_power, Vmax_range, Vy, ceiling - Range–endurance–speed performance curves

Propulsion (aerisplane.propulsion) - Motor/propeller/battery/ESC operating-point solver - Outputs: thrust, RPM, current, motor efficiency, propulsive efficiency, battery endurance, C-rate, over-current flag

MDO (aerisplane.mdo) - MDOProblem: design variables, constraints, objectives - String-path design variable syntax ("wings[0].xsecs[1].chord") - Evaluation caching keyed on design vector - SciPy drivers: scipy_de, scipy_minimize, scipy_shgo - pygmo drivers: pygmo_de, pygmo_sade, pygmo_nsga2 - Checkpoint/resume - Sensitivity analysis: finite-difference gradients, normalized ranking

Catalog (aerisplane.catalog) - 20 brushless motors, 15 LiPo batteries, 10 propellers, 10 servos - 2175 airfoil .dat files - list_motors(), list_batteries(), list_propellers(), list_servos()

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: AerisPlane is in early development. Issues and pull requests are welcome.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/KasparStepan/AerisPlane.git
cd AerisPlane
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Running tests
docs.text:
pytest tests/
pytest tests/test_aero/   # aerodynamics only

docs.title: Linting
docs.text: ruff check src/

docs.title: Building the docs locally
docs.text:
pip install -e ".[docs]"
mkdocs serve

Open http://127.0.0.1:8000 to preview the site.

docs.title: Branch naming
docs.text:
• feature/<name> — new functionality
  • fix/<name> — bug fixes
  • docs/<name> — documentation only

Submit a pull request against main. Please include a brief description of what changed and why.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: A five-minute introduction to the AerisPlane workflow.

docs.title: 1. Define geometry
docs.text:
An aircraft is built from Wing and Fuselage objects composed of cross-sections. The main wing, horizontal tail, and vertical tail are all Wing objects — there is no separate tail class.

import aerisplane as ap

# Main wing with two cross-sections (root and tip)
wing = ap.Wing(
    name="main_wing",
    symmetric=True,   # mirror across y=0 plane
    xsecs=[
        ap.WingXSec(xyz_le=[0.00, 0.00, 0.00], chord=0.28, airfoil=ap.Airfoil(name="ag35")),
        ap.WingXSec(xyz_le=[0.07, 1.20, 0.05], chord=0.14, twist=-2.0, airfoil=ap.Airfoil(name="ag35")),
    ],
)

# Horizontal tail with elevator
htail = ap.Wing(
    name="htail",
    symmetric=True,
    xsecs=[
        ap.WingXSec(xyz_le=[0.95, 0.00, 0.00], chord=0.15, airfoil=ap.Airfoil(name="naca0012")),
        ap.WingXSec(xyz_le=[0.98, 0.40, 0.00], chord=0.10, airfoil=ap.Airfoil(name="naca0012")),
    ],
    control_surfaces=[
        ap.ControlSurface(name="elevator", span_start=0.0, span_end=1.0,
                          chord_fraction=0.35, symmetric=True),
    ],
)

aircraft = ap.Aircraft(name="MyPlane", wings=[wing, htail])

Coordinate convention: xyz_le = [x, y, z] is the leading-edge position in aircraft frame. x points aft, y points right, z points up. For symmetric wings, define the right side (y ≥ 0) only.

docs.title: 2. Run an aerodynamic analysis
docs.text:
from aerisplane.aero import analyze, plot_geometry

# Plot the geometry to verify it looks right
plot_geometry(aircraft, style="three_view")

# Run VLM at a single operating point
cond = ap.FlightCondition(velocity=16.0, altitude=0.0, alpha=4.0)
result = analyze(aircraft, cond, method="vlm")
result.report()

Available methods: "vlm" (fast, inviscid), "lifting_line" (viscous with NeuralFoil), "nonlinear_lifting_line" (captures stall), "aero_buildup" (semi-empirical).

docs.title: 3. Check weights
docs.text:
from aerisplane.weights import analyze as weight_analysis

wr = weight_analysis(aircraft)
wr.report()
wr.plot_mass_breakdown()

docs.title: 4. Run the full discipline chain
docs.text:
from aerisplane import weights, stability

wr = weights.analyze(aircraft)
aircraft.xyz_ref = wr.cg.tolist()   # set moment reference to CG

sr = stability.analyze(aircraft, cond, wr)
print(f"Static margin: {sr.static_margin:.1%} MAC")
print(f"Cm_alpha:      {sr.Cm_alpha:.4f} 1/deg  ({'stable' if sr.Cm_alpha < 0 else 'UNSTABLE'})")

See the Tutorials for deeper worked examples covering every discipline module.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python ≥ 3.10
  • numpy, scipy, matplotlib, neuralfoil (installed automatically)

docs.title: Standard install
docs.text: pip install aerisplane

docs.title: Editable install (development)
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/KasparStepan/AerisPlane.git
cd AerisPlane
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Optional extras
docs.text:
pip install aerisplane[oas]          # OpenAeroStruct for detailed structural analysis
pip install aerisplane[optimize]     # pygmo global optimizers
pip install aerisplane[interactive]  # Plotly interactive flow visualisation
pip install aerisplane[all]          # everything

docs.title: Aerodynamics API
docs.text: Module: aerisplane.aero

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
import aerisplane as ap

# Build aircraft (see core API for full geometry definition)
aircraft = ap.Aircraft(name="my_plane", wings=[main_wing], fuselages=[fuse])

# Define flight condition
condition = ap.FlightCondition(velocity=20.0, altitude=500.0, alpha=4.0)

# Run analysis
result = ap.aero.analyze(aircraft, condition, method="vlm")
result.report()

docs.title: analyze()
docs.text:
aerisplane.aero.analyze(
    aircraft,
    condition,
    method="vlm",
    backend="native",
    spanwise_resolution=8,
    chordwise_resolution=4,
    model_size="medium",
    verbose=False,
) -> AeroResult

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description aircraft Aircraft — Aircraft geometry condition FlightCondition — Operating point method str "vlm" Solver — see table below backend str "native" "native" or "aerosandbox" spanwise_resolution int 8 Spanwise panels per wing section (VLM, LL) chordwise_resolution int 4 Chordwise panels per section (VLM only) model_size str "medium" NeuralFoil model size (LL, NLL, AeroBuildup) verbose bool False Print solver progress

docs.title: Solver methods
docs.text:
Method string Physics Stall Viscous Notes "vlm" Inviscid, linear ✗ ✗ Fast. Correct CL, Cm, Cl, Cn. All drag is CDi. "lifting_line" Viscous, linearised CL partial ✅ Requires neuralfoil "nonlinear_lifting_line" Viscous, nonlinear CL ✅ ✅ Fixed-point iteration. Requires neuralfoil "aero_buildup" Semi-empirical ✅ ✅ Jorgensen fuselage + NeuralFoil wings. Requires neuralfoil

model_size options (NeuralFoil): "xxsmall", "xsmall", "small", "medium", "large", "xlarge", "xxlarge". Larger models are more accurate but slower.

docs.title: FlightCondition
docs.text:
aerisplane.FlightCondition(
    velocity,           # True airspeed [m/s]
    altitude=0.0,       # Geometric altitude above MSL [m]
    alpha=0.0,          # Angle of attack [deg]
    beta=0.0,           # Sideslip angle [deg]
    p=0.0,              # Roll rate [rad/s]
    q=0.0,              # Pitch rate [rad/s]
    r=0.0,              # Yaw rate [rad/s]
    deflections={},     # Control surface deflections [deg], keyed by surface name
)

docs.title: Control surface deflections
docs.text:
Deflections are passed through FlightCondition.deflections — a plain dict mapping surface name to angle in degrees. The surface name must match the ControlSurface.name field on the wing.

# Elevator down 5°, aileron right side down 10°
condition = ap.FlightCondition(
    velocity=20.0,
    altitude=500.0,
    alpha=3.0,
    deflections={"elevator": -5.0, "aileron": 10.0},
)

Sign conventions: - Positive = trailing edge down (elevator nose-up, flap lift-up) - For ControlSurface(symmetric=False) (aileron): positive deflects the right panel trailing-edge down and the left panel trailing-edge up

docs.title: Useful methods
docs.text:
cond.dynamic_pressure()           # q = 0.5 * rho * V^2  [Pa]
cond.reynolds_number(chord)       # Re = rho * V * c / mu
cond.mach()                       # Mach number at altitude
cond.density()                    # Air density [kg/m³]

docs.title: AeroResult
docs.text: Returned by analyze(). All quantities are in SI units.

docs.title: Forces and coefficients
docs.text: Field Unit Description CL — Lift coefficient CD — Total drag coefficient CY — Side force coefficient CDi — Induced drag coefficient (None for LL/NLL/AeroBuildup) CDp — Profile drag coefficient (None for VLM/LL/NLL) L N Lift force (wind axes) D N Drag force (wind axes) Y N Side force (wind axes)

docs.title: Moments and coefficients
docs.text:
Field Unit Description Cl — Rolling moment coefficient (body axes) Cm — Pitching moment coefficient (body axes) Cn — Yawing moment coefficient (body axes) l_b N·m Rolling moment (body axes) m_b N·m Pitching moment (body axes) n_b N·m Yawing moment (body axes)

Moments are taken about aircraft.xyz_ref (default: origin). Set xyz_ref to the CG location for stability analysis.

docs.title: Force/moment vectors
docs.text: Field Description F_g Force vector in geometry axes [Fx, Fy, Fz] [N] F_b Force vector in body axes [N] F_w Force vector in wind axes [N] M_g Moment vector in geometry axes [Mx, My, Mz] [N·m] M_b Moment vector in body axes [N·m] M_w Moment vector in wind axes [N·m]

docs.title: Operating condition (echoed)
docs.text: Field Unit Description alpha deg Angle of attack beta deg Sideslip angle velocity m/s True airspeed altitude m Geometric altitude dynamic_pressure Pa q = 0.5 ρ V² reynolds_number — Re based on reference chord

docs.title: Reference geometry (echoed)
docs.text: Field Unit Description s_ref m² Reference area (main wing area) c_ref m Reference chord (MAC of main wing) b_ref m Reference span (main wing span)

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
result.report()
Prints a formatted summary table to stdout.

AeroResult.plot_polar(results, show=True, save_path=None)
Static method. Plot CL, CD, and Cm polars from a list of AeroResult objects (typically an alpha sweep).

result.plot_spanwise_loading(show=True, save_path=None)
Plot spanwise section-CL distribution. VLM results only (requires _solver to be a VortexLatticeMethod instance).

docs.title: plot_geometry()
docs.text:
aerisplane.aero.plot_geometry(
    aircraft,
    style="three_view",   # "three_view" | "wireframe"
    show=True,
    save_path=None,
)

Visualise aircraft geometry. "three_view" produces a 4-panel engineering drawing (top, front, side, isometric). "wireframe" produces a single 3-D matplotlib view.

docs.title: Alpha sweep with VLM
docs.text:
import numpy as np
import aerisplane as ap
from aerisplane.aero import analyze

condition_base = ap.FlightCondition(velocity=20.0, altitude=500.0)
alphas = np.linspace(-4, 14, 10)
results = [
    analyze(aircraft, ap.FlightCondition(velocity=20.0, altitude=500.0, alpha=a))
    for a in alphas
]

ap.aero.AeroResult.plot_polar(results)

docs.title: Control surface deflection sweep
docs.text:
deflections = np.linspace(-20, 20, 9)
results = [
    analyze(
        aircraft,
        ap.FlightCondition(velocity=20.0, altitude=500.0, alpha=4.0,
                           deflections={"elevator": d}),
    )
    for d in deflections
]
cms = [r.Cm for r in results]

docs.title: Accessing raw solver data (VLM)
docs.text:
result = analyze(aircraft, condition, method="vlm")
vlm = result._solver          # VortexLatticeMethod instance
gammas = vlm.vortex_strengths # (N,) vortex strength array
panels = vlm.collocation_points  # (N, 3) panel collocation points

docs.title: aerisplane.aero.analyze(aircraft, condition, method='vlm', spanwise_resolution=8, chordwise_resolution=4, model_size='medium', verbose=False)
docs.text:
Run an aerodynamic analysis.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default aircraft Aircraft

aerisplane aircraft definition.

required condition FlightCondition

Operating point (velocity, altitude, alpha, beta, deflections).

required method str

Solver method: "vlm" (default), "lifting_line", "nonlinear_lifting_line", "aero_buildup".

'vlm' spanwise_resolution int

Spanwise panels / stations per wing section. Applies to VLM and LiftingLine. Default 8.

8 chordwise_resolution int

Chordwise panels per section. VLM only. Default 4.

4 model_size str

NeuralFoil model size for LiftingLine and AeroBuildup. Options: "xxsmall" … "xxlarge". Default "medium".

'medium' verbose bool

Print solver progress. Default False.

False

Returns:

Type Description AeroResult

Full aerodynamic result including forces, moments, and coefficients.

docs.title: aerisplane.aero.result.AeroResult dataclass
docs.text:
Full aerodynamic analysis result from a single operating point.

Forces are in wind axes (L, D, Y). Moments are in body axes (l_b, m_b, n_b). Coefficients follow the same axis convention.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default method str

Solver used: "vlm", "lifting_line", "nonlinear_lifting_line", "aero_buildup".

required Forces (wind axes) [N]

L : float Lift force [N]. D : float Drag force [N]. Y : float Side force [N].

Moments (body axes) [Nm]

l_b : float Rolling moment [Nm]. Positive = roll right. m_b : float Pitching moment [Nm]. Positive = pitch up. n_b : float Yawing moment [Nm]. Positive = nose right.

Force vectors [N]

F_g, F_b, F_w : list[float] [x, y, z] force components in geometry, body, wind axes. M_g, M_b, M_w : list[float] [x, y, z] moment components in geometry, body, wind axes.

Coefficients

CL, CD, CY : float Force coefficients (wind axes). Cl, Cm, Cn : float Moment coefficients (body axes). CDi : float or None Induced drag coefficient. None when not available. CDp : float or None Parasitic (viscous) drag coefficient. Available from aero_buildup only.

Operating condition (echoed)

alpha : float Angle of attack [deg]. beta : float Sideslip angle [deg]. velocity : float True airspeed [m/s]. altitude : float Altitude [m]. dynamic_pressure : float Dynamic pressure q [Pa]. reynolds_number : float Reynolds number based on reference chord.

Reference geometry (echoed)

s_ref : float Reference area [m^2]. c_ref : float Reference chord [m]. b_ref : float Reference span [m].

docs.title: CL_over_CD property
docs.text:
Return the lift-to-drag ratio (CL / CD).

Returns zero if CD is zero or negative to avoid division errors.

docs.title: report()
docs.text: Print a formatted summary of the aerodynamic result.

docs.title: plot_polar(results, show=True, save_path=None) staticmethod
docs.text:
Plot a CL-alpha, CD-alpha, Cm-alpha polar from a list of AeroResult objects.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default results list of AeroResult

Results at different alpha values (sorted by alpha automatically).

required show bool

Whether to call plt.show().

True save_path str or None

If given, saves the figure to this path.

None

docs.title: plot_spanwise_loading(show=True, save_path=None)
docs.text:
Plot spanwise Cl distribution from a VLM result.

Uses per-panel data stored from the AeroSandbox VLM solver. Only available when method='vlm' and the result was produced by aerisplane.aero.analyze().

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default show bool

Whether to call plt.show().

True save_path str or None

If given, save the figure to this path.

None

Raises:

Type Description RuntimeError

If solver data is not available (non-VLM methods or externally constructed AeroResult).

docs.title: plot_surface_pressure(show=True, save_path=None)
docs.text: Cp on wing panels — 3-D and top-down views. Requires method='vlm'.

docs.title: plot_streamlines(plane='xz', x_slice=None, show=True, save_path=None)
docs.text:
Streamlines on a 2-D slice of the VLM flow field.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default plane str

"xz" — symmetry plane, shows upwash/downwash. "yz" — rear cross-section, shows tip vortices.

'xz' x_slice float or None

x position [m] for the "yz" plane. Defaults to 2 chord lengths behind the trailing edge.

None

docs.title: plot_flow(show=True, save_path=None)
docs.text: Combined 4-panel flow figure: Cp top-down, Cp 3-D, XZ streamlines, YZ tip-vortex crossflow. Requires method='vlm'.

docs.title: plot_interactive(n_streamlines=40, show=True, save_path=None)
docs.text:
Interactive Plotly 3-D figure: wing surface coloured by ΔCp with 3-D streamlines traced through the full velocity field.

Requires plotly (installed by default with most scientific Python environments). Opens in the browser or renders inline in Jupyter.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default n_streamlines int

Number of streamlines to trace (seeded upstream). Default 40.

40 show bool

Call fig.show().

True save_path str or None

If given, write an interactive HTML file to this path.

None

docs.title: Catalog — aerisplane.catalog
docs.text: Hardware database of real-world components for direct use in PropulsionSystem and Aircraft definitions.

docs.title: Browsing the catalog
docs.text:
from aerisplane.catalog import list_motors, list_batteries, list_propellers, list_servos

motors = list_motors()
for m in sorted(motors, key=lambda x: x.kv):
    print(f"{m.name:40s}  KV={m.kv:5.0f}  I_max={m.max_current:.0f}A  mass={m.mass*1000:.0f}g")

docs.title: Using catalog items
docs.text:
from aerisplane.catalog.motors    import sunnysky_x2216_1250
from aerisplane.catalog.batteries import tattu_4s_5200
from aerisplane.catalog.propellers import apc_10x4_7sf
from aerisplane.core.propulsion import ESC, PropulsionSystem

propulsion = PropulsionSystem(
    motor=sunnysky_x2216_1250,
    propeller=apc_10x4_7sf,
    battery=tattu_4s_5200,
    esc=ESC(name="generic_60A", max_current=60.0, mass=0.025),
)

docs.title: Airfoils
docs.text:
The airfoil catalog contains 2175 .dat files. Load by name:

import aerisplane as ap
af = ap.Airfoil(name="ag35")       # load from catalog
af = ap.Airfoil(name="naca2412")   # generated analytically (NACA 4-digit)

docs.title: aerisplane.catalog.list_motors()
docs.text:
Return all motors in the catalog.

Returns:

Type Description list of Motor

All :class:~aerisplane.core.propulsion.Motor instances in aerisplane.catalog.motors.

Examples:

>>> from aerisplane.catalog import list_motors
>>> motors = list_motors()
>>> for m in motors:
...     print(f"{m.name}  KV={m.kv}")

docs.text:

All :class:~aerisplane.core.propulsion.Battery instances in aerisplane.catalog.batteries.

docs.title: Control — aerisplane.control
docs.text:
Control authority analysis: roll rate, pitch authority, rudder authority, and servo loads at a given flight condition. Computes control derivatives by forward finite-differencing the aerodynamic solver.

Requires ControlSurface objects on the wings and a completed stability.analyze().

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
import aerisplane as ap
from aerisplane.weights import analyze as weight_analyze
from aerisplane.stability import analyze as stab_analyze
from aerisplane.control import analyze

wr = weight_analyze(aircraft)
aircraft.xyz_ref = wr.cg.tolist()

cond = ap.FlightCondition(velocity=18.0, altitude=0.0, alpha=4.0)
sr = stab_analyze(aircraft, cond, wr)
result = analyze(aircraft, cond, wr, sr, aero_method="vlm")
print(result.report())

docs.title: aerisplane.control.analyze(aircraft, condition, weight_result, stability_result, aero_method='vlm', **aero_kwargs)
docs.text:
Run a full control authority analysis.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default aircraft Aircraft

Aircraft geometry definition.

required condition FlightCondition

Operating point (velocity, altitude, alpha, beta).

required weight_result WeightResult

Weight analysis result (mass, CG, inertia tensor).

required stability_result StabilityResult

Stability analysis result (Cn_beta for crosswind calculation).

required aero_method str

Aero solver method (default "vlm").

'vlm' **aero_kwargs

Additional keyword arguments passed to aero.analyze().

{}

Returns:

Type Description ControlResult

Complete control authority analysis result.

max_crosswind float

Max crosswind for coordinated flight [m/s].

docs.title: report()
docs.text: Formatted control authority report.

docs.title: plot()
docs.text:
Control authority summary plot.

Returns a matplotlib Figure with: 1. Authority bar chart (roll, pitch, yaw) with threshold line 2. Servo torque margin for each surface with servo

docs.title: Interpreting authority values
docs.text:
aileron_authority, elevator_authority, rudder_authority are normalised [0, 1]:

  • 1.0 — surface meets or exceeds the reference requirement
  • < 1.0 — surface is undersized relative to the reference requirement

Reference values used internally: - Roll: 60 deg/s minimum roll rate - Crosswind: 5 m/s crosswind capability - Elevator: ability to trim over a ±10° alpha range

docs.title: Core — aerisplane.core
docs.text: The core module contains the geometry and component dataclasses used by every discipline module. It depends only on numpy — no solvers or backends.

docs.title: Coordinate system
docs.text:
All positions use the aircraft body frame:

  • x — positive aft (toward the tail)
  • y — positive right (starboard wing)
  • z — positive up

xyz_le on WingXSec and Wing is the leading-edge position in this frame. For symmetric wings, define the right side (y ≥ 0) only; the left side is mirrored automatically.

docs.text: Total fuselage length [m].

docs.title: volume(_sectional=False)
docs.text:
Internal volume [m^3].

Uses the Prismatoid formula per section, which is exact for frustums (tapered cylinders) and more accurate than trapz for nose/tail cones:

V_section = (x_b - x_a) / 3 * (A_a + A_b + sqrt(A_a * A_b))

docs.title: aerisplane.core.fuselage.FuselageXSec dataclass
docs.text:
A cross-section of a fuselage at a given axial station.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default x float

Axial position from nose [m]. 0 = nose tip.

required width float

Cross-section width in the y-direction [m].

0.0 height float

Cross-section height in the z-direction [m].

0.0 shape float

Superellipse exponent. Controls cross-section shape: shape=1.0 → diamond (45° rotated square) shape=2.0 → circle / ellipse (default) shape=5.0 → rounded rectangle shape→∞ → rectangle Use 2.0 for aerodynamic bodies, 4–8 for fuselages with flat sides.

2.0 radius float or None

Backward-compat convenience: sets width = height = 2 * radius, shape = 2.0. Use width/height directly for non-circular cross-sections.

None

docs.title: area()
docs.text:
Cross-sectional area [m^2].

Uses closed-form superellipse approximation (error < 0.6% for shape >= 1):

area = width * height / (shape^-1.8718 + 1)

Exact for shape=1 (diamond) and shape=2 (circle/ellipse).

docs.title: perimeter()
docs.text:
Cross-section perimeter [m].

Closed-form approximation derived by symbolic regression (error < 0.2%).

docs.title: equivalent_radius(preserve='area')
docs.text:
Equivalent circular radius [m] that preserves area or perimeter.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default preserve str

"area" : radius = sqrt(area / pi) "perimeter" : radius = perimeter / (2 * pi)

'area'

Airfoil name, e.g. "ag35", "naca2412", "e387". Used for catalog lookup and plot labels.

required coordinates ndarray of shape (N, 2) or None

Explicit (x, y) coordinate array in Selig format (upper surface first, x from 0 to 1 and back to 0). If None, loaded from catalog by name.

None

Examples:

>>> af = Airfoil(name="ag35")         # loads from catalog
>>> af = Airfoil(name="naca2412")     # generated analytically

Calls the NeuralFoil neural-network-based airfoil model to obtain CL, CD, and CM at the given operating condition. NeuralFoil must be installed (pip install neuralfoil).

'large' control_surfaces list or None

Ignored for now (Phase 4 will wire in deflections).

None

docs.title: aerisplane.core.control_surface.ControlSurface dataclass
docs.text:
A hinged control surface on a wing.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default name str

Surface name used to key FlightCondition.deflections. Conventional names: "elevator", "aileron", "rudder", "flap".

required span_start float

Start of the surface as a fraction of the wing semi-span [0, 1].

required span_end float

End of the surface as a fraction of the wing semi-span [0, 1].

required chord_fraction float

Hinge chord fraction [0, 1]. 0.25 means the surface occupies the aft 25% of the local chord.

required symmetric bool

If True, deflects the same way on both sides (elevator, flap). If False, the sign is mirrored on the left side (aileron).

True max_deflection float

Maximum deflection magnitude [deg]. Used for authority calculations.

25.0 Notes

Sign convention: positive deflection = trailing edge down. For symmetric=False (ailerons): positive = right side TE-down / left side TE-up.

Examples:

>>> elevator = ControlSurface(name="elevator", span_start=0.0, span_end=1.0,
...                            chord_fraction=0.35, symmetric=True, max_deflection=25.0)
>>> aileron = ControlSurface(name="aileron", span_start=0.5, span_end=0.9,
...                           chord_fraction=0.25, symmetric=False, max_deflection=20.0)

docs.title: freestream_velocity_geometry_axes()
docs.text:
Freestream velocity vector in geometry axes [m/s].

Geometry axes: x downstream, y right, z up. Wind axes: x upstream (into the wind), y right, z up.

The rotation from wind to geometry is: flip x/z (pi about y), then rotate by -alpha about y, then by +beta about z.

docs.title: rotation_velocity_geometry_axes(points, p=0.0, q=0.0, r=0.0)
docs.text:
Velocity at points due to aircraft rotation [m/s].

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default points (N, 3) array

Points in geometry axes.

required p float

Roll, pitch, yaw rates [rad/s] in body axes.

0.0 q float

Roll, pitch, yaw rates [rad/s] in body axes.

0.0 r float

Roll, pitch, yaw rates [rad/s] in body axes.

0.0

Returns:

Type Description (N, 3) array

Velocity the wing sees at each point (opposite of point velocity).

docs.title: convert_axes(x_from, y_from, z_from, from_axes, to_axes)
docs.text:
Convert a vector between geometry / body / wind / stability axes.

Follows the conventions in Drela, Flight Vehicle Aerodynamics, §6.2.2.

Geometry axes: x downstream, y right, z up. Body axes: x forward, y right, z down. Wind axes: x into wind, y right, z "up" (lift direction). Stability axes: body axes rotated by alpha about y.

docs.title: Control surface deflections
docs.text:
Deflections are passed through FlightCondition.deflections — a dict mapping surface name to angle in degrees. The surface name must match ControlSurface.name on the wing.

cond = ap.FlightCondition(
    velocity=18.0,
    altitude=100.0,
    alpha=3.5,
    deflections={"elevator": -5.0, "aileron": 10.0},
)

docs.title: MDO — aerisplane.mdo
docs.text: Multidisciplinary optimisation orchestration. Defines the problem (design variables, constraints, objective), evaluates the full discipline chain for each candidate design, and drives external optimisers.

docs.title: Minimal example
docs.text:
import aerisplane as ap
from aerisplane.mdo import MDOProblem, DesignVar, Constraint, Objective
from aerisplane.mission.segments import Mission, Cruise, Loiter

# ... define aircraft with propulsion ...

mission = Mission(
    start_altitude=0.0,
    segments=[
        Cruise(name="cruise", velocity=18.0, altitude=100.0, distance=8000.0),
        Loiter(name="loiter", velocity=16.0, altitude=100.0, duration=900.0),
    ]
)

problem = MDOProblem(
    aircraft=aircraft,
    condition=ap.FlightCondition(velocity=18.0, altitude=100.0),
    design_variables=[
        DesignVar("wings[0].xsecs[1].chord",     lower=0.08, upper=0.22, scale=0.15),
        DesignVar("wings[0].xsecs[1].xyz_le[1]", lower=0.8,  upper=1.8,  scale=1.2),
    ],
    constraints=[
        Constraint("structures.wings[0].bending_margin", lower=0.0),
        Constraint("stability.static_margin", lower=0.05, upper=0.20),
    ],
    objective=Objective("mission.total_energy", maximize=False),
    mission=mission,
)

result = problem.optimize(method="scipy_de", options={"maxiter": 200, "seed": 42})
result.report()

docs.title: Design variable paths
docs.text:
Design variables use dot-bracket path syntax into the Aircraft object:

Example path What it controls "wings[0].xsecs[1].chord" Tip chord of first wing "wings[0].xsecs[1].xyz_le[1]" Semi-span (y of tip leading edge) "wings[0].xsecs[0].twist" Root twist angle "wings[1].xsecs[0].chord" Root chord of second wing (htail)

Paths are validated at MDOProblem construction time — a ValueError is raised immediately if a path does not resolve on the aircraft.

docs.title: Constraint and objective paths
docs.text: Prefix Example Notes "aero." "aero.CL" Fields of AeroResult "weights." "weights.total_mass" Fields of WeightResult "structures." "structures.wings[0].bending_margin" Fields of WingStructureResult "stability." "stability.static_margin" Fields of StabilityResult "control." "control.aileron_authority" Fields of ControlResult "mission." "mission.total_energy" Fields of MissionResult "propulsion." "propulsion.c_rate" Fields of PropulsionResult

docs.title: Optimizer methods
docs.text:
Method Driver Description "scipy_de" SciPy Differential Evolution — global, robust, handles integers "scipy_minimize" SciPy Local gradient-free or gradient-based "scipy_shgo" SciPy Simplicial Homology Global Optimisation "pygmo_de" pygmo Differential Evolution (requires pip install pygmo) "pygmo_sade" pygmo Self-Adaptive DE "pygmo_nsga2" pygmo Multi-objective NSGA-II

Recommended default: "scipy_de".

docs.title: Checkpoint and resume
docs.text:
result = problem.optimize(
    method="scipy_de",
    checkpoint_path="runs/opt_run1",
    checkpoint_interval=50,
)
# Resume: pass the same checkpoint_path — automatically resumes
result = problem.optimize(
    method="scipy_de",
    checkpoint_path="runs/opt_run1",
)

docs.title: Sensitivity analysis
docs.text:
sens = problem.sensitivity(result.x_optimal)
print(sens.report())

docs.title: aerisplane.mdo.problem.MDOProblem
docs.text:
Multidisciplinary optimisation problem.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default aircraft Aircraft

Baseline aircraft. A deep copy is stored.

required condition FlightCondition

Reference flight condition. Provides velocity, altitude, beta. Alpha is taken from the alpha parameter (fixed) or solved for trim (alpha=None).

None design_variables list of DesignVar None constraints list of Constraint None objective Objective or list of Objective None mission Mission or None None airfoil_pools dict or None

Mapping wing_path -> AirfoilPool.

None alpha float or None

Fixed alpha [deg]. None -> auto-trim each evaluation.

None aero_method str 'vlm' load_factor float

Limit maneuver load factor for structures.analyze(). Default 3.5.

3.5 extra_disciplines tuple of str () skip_disciplines tuple of str ()

docs.title: validate()
docs.text: Resolve all DesignVar paths against the aircraft. Raise ValueError on bad paths.

docs.title: simulate()
docs.text:
Run the discipline chain once at the baseline design. No optimization.

Useful for initial sizing: inspect discipline results before launching an optimizer. Results are cached — calling simulate() twice is free.

Returns:

Type Description dict

Mapping discipline name → result object, e.g.::

results = problem.simulate()
print(results["aero"].CL_over_CD)
print(results["weights"].total_mass)
results["aero"].report()

docs.title: get_bounds()
docs.text: Return (lower, upper) bound arrays.

docs.title: evaluate(x_scaled)
docs.text:
Run the full discipline chain for design vector x_scaled.

Returns dict with keys: objective, constraint_values, results, aircraft, condition, elapsed.

docs.title: objective_function(x_scaled)
docs.text: Return scalar objective for single-objective optimisers.

docs.title: constraint_functions(x_scaled)
docs.text: Return violation vector (<= 0 means satisfied).

docs.title: save_cache(path)
docs.text: Persist evaluation cache to a pickle file.

docs.title: load_cache(path)
docs.text: Load a previously saved cache, merging with existing entries.

docs.title: optimize(method='scipy_de', options=None, verbose=1, report_interval=None, log_path=None, callback=None, checkpoint_path=None, checkpoint_interval=None, plot_path=None)
docs.text:
Run optimisation and return an OptimizationResult.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default method str

One of "scipy_de", "scipy_minimize", "scipy_shgo", "pymoo_de", "pymoo_nsga2", "pymoo_nsga3", "pymoo_pso".

'scipy_de' options dict or None

Passed directly to the underlying optimiser. scipy_de: maxiter, popsize, seed, tol, ... pymoo: pop_size, n_gen, seed, ...

None verbose int

0 / False — silent. 1 / True — tqdm progress bar showing eval count, current and best objective, elapsed time (default). 2 — bar + per-generation table of best design variable values with initial value and delta.

1 report_interval int or None

Print a detailed summary every N evaluations. None → off.

None log_path str or None

Path for CSV log (appended if file exists). None → no file.

None callback callable or None

Called with OptimisationSnapshot after each evaluation. Return "stop" to terminate early.

None checkpoint_path str or None

Base path (no extension) for checkpoint files. Existing checkpoint is loaded and run resumes automatically.

None checkpoint_interval int or None

Save checkpoint every N evaluations. None → every popsize evaluations.

None plot_path str or None

If supplied, the convergence + design-variable figure is saved to this path after optimisation completes. Accepts any matplotlib-supported extension: .png, .pdf, .svg, etc. Parent directory must exist.

None

Returns:

Type Description OptimizationResult

docs.title: sensitivity(x, step=0.0001)
docs.text:
Compute finite-difference sensitivity at a given design point.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default x ndarray — design vector in scaled optimizer space required step float — forward finite-difference step in scaled space 0.0001

Returns:

Type Description SensitivityResult

Gradients and normalized sensitivities ranked by objective influence.

Dot path into the results dict, e.g. "stability.static_margin" or "structures.wings[0].bending_margin".

E.g. "mission.endurance_s" or "weights.total_mass".

True → maximise (default). False → minimise.

True scale float

docs.title: report()
docs.text:
Formatted plain-text optimisation summary.

Sections: - Objective: initial, optimal, % change, eval count, constraint status - Design Variables: table of path / initial / optimal values - Pareto Front: solution count (only if pareto_front is not None)

docs.title: plot()
docs.text:
Return a 1×2 matplotlib figure.

Left panel: convergence history (objective vs evaluation number), with a rolling average overlay when history has ≥10 points. Right panel: horizontal bar chart comparing initial and optimal design variable values.

docs.title: plot_pareto()
docs.text:
Plot the Pareto front for multi-objective results.

Returns a scatter figure of objective 1 vs objective 2. Returns None and emits a UserWarning if pareto_front is None (single-objective run).

docs.title: aerisplane.mdo.sensitivity.SensitivityResult dataclass
docs.text:
Sensitivity analysis result.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default dvar_paths list of str — design variable paths required grad_objective ndarray shape (n,) — dObjective/dx_i required grad_constraints dict[str, ndarray] — dConstraint/dx_i per constraint path required normalized_objective ndarray shape (n,) — |dObj/dx_i| * |x_i / obj| required normalized_constraints dict[str, ndarray] — same for each constraint required x ndarray — design vector at evaluation required objective_value float — objective value at x required step_size float — finite-difference step used required

docs.title: report()
docs.text: Ranked sensitivity table as a human-readable string.

docs.title: plot()
docs.text: Horizontal bar chart of normalized objective sensitivities.

docs.title: Mission & Flight Performance API
docs.text: Module: aerisplane.mission

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
import aerisplane as ap
from aerisplane import weights, mission
from aerisplane.mission.segments import Mission, Climb, Cruise, Loiter, Descent, Return

# 1. Build aircraft with propulsion system
aircraft = ap.Aircraft(name="my_uav", wings=[main_wing], fuselages=[fuse],
                       propulsion=propulsion)

# 2. Weight analysis (needed for CG and mass)
weight_result = weights.analyze(aircraft)

# 3. Flight envelope (power curves, speeds, climb, glide, endurance)
env = mission.envelope(aircraft, weight_result)
print(env.report())
env.plot()

# 4. Mission energy budget
my_mission = Mission(segments=[
    Climb(to_altitude=100, climb_rate=2.0, velocity=12.0),
    Cruise(distance=5000, velocity=15.0, altitude=100.0),
    Descent(to_altitude=0.0),
])
result = mission.analyze(aircraft, weight_result, my_mission)
print(result.report())
result.plot()

docs.title: mission.performance()
docs.text:
mission.performance(aircraft, weight_result, altitude=0.0, aero_method="vlm") -> DragPolar

Fits a parabolic drag polar CD = CD0 + k·CL² from VLM evaluations at 3 speeds.

docs.title: mission.envelope()
docs.text:
mission.envelope(aircraft, weight_result, CL_max=1.4, aero_method="vlm") -> EnvelopeResult

Computes the full flight performance envelope across altitudes (0–3000 m by default).

docs.title: mission.analyze()
docs.text:
mission.analyze(aircraft, weight_result, mission, aero_method="vlm") -> MissionResult

Runs a mission energy budget analysis. For each segment, computes duration, distance, and energy consumed.

docs.title: Performance functions
docs.text: Module: aerisplane.mission.performance

docs.title: Drag polar
docs.text:
fit_drag_polar(aircraft, weight_result, altitude=0.0, aero_method="vlm",
               speeds=(10.0, 15.0, 20.0)) -> DragPolar

Fits CD = CD0 + k·CL² by running the aero solver at several speeds and doing a least-squares fit. Since VLM is inviscid, parasitic drag (CD0) is estimated from turbulent flat-plate skin friction when the fitted value is too small.

docs.title: DragPolar dataclass
docs.text: Field Type Description CD0 float Zero-lift drag coefficient k float Induced drag factor (k = 1/(π·AR·e)) S_ref float Reference wing area [m²] Method Returns Description cd(cl) float Total CD at given CL ld_max() float Maximum lift-to-drag ratio cl_for_ld_max() float CL at max L/D cl_for_min_power() float CL at minimum power required

docs.title: Power curves
docs.text:
power_required(velocity, polar, mass, altitude=0.0) -> float    # [W]
power_available(propulsion, velocity, altitude=0.0, throttle=1.0) -> float  # [W]
  • power_required: P_R = 0.5·ρ·S·CD0·V³ + 2·k·W²/(ρ·S·V)
  • power_available: thrust × velocity at the motor-prop operating point

docs.title: Characteristic speeds
docs.text:
stall_speed(mass, S, CL_max=1.4, altitude=0.0) -> float              # [m/s]
best_range_speed(polar, mass, altitude=0.0) -> float                  # [m/s]
best_endurance_speed(polar, mass, altitude=0.0) -> float              # [m/s]
max_level_speed(polar, mass, propulsion, altitude=0.0) -> float|None  # [m/s]
Speed Formula Meaning V_stall √(2W / (ρ·S·CL_max)) Minimum flight speed V* (best range) V at CL = √(CD0/k) Maximum L/D, longest range V_mp (best endurance) 0.76 × V* Minimum power, longest time aloft V_max Largest V where P_A ≥ P_R Maximum level flight speed

docs.title: Climb performance
docs.text:
rate_of_climb(velocity, polar, mass, propulsion, altitude=0.0) -> float       # [m/s]
max_rate_of_climb(polar, mass, propulsion, altitude=0.0) -> (ROC_max, V_y)    # [m/s]

ROC = (P_A − P_R) / W. Returns the excess-power climb rate.

docs.title: Glide performance
docs.text:
glide_range(polar, from_altitude) -> float                    # [m]
glide_performance(polar, mass, altitude=0.0) -> GlidePerformance

docs.title: GlidePerformance dataclass
docs.text: Field Type Description best_glide_ratio float L/D max best_glide_speed float Speed for best glide [m/s] min_sink_speed float Speed for minimum sink rate [m/s] min_sink_rate float Minimum vertical descent rate [m/s]

docs.title: Endurance and range
docs.text:
max_endurance(polar, mass, propulsion, altitude=0.0, eta_total=None) -> float  # [seconds]
max_range(polar, mass, propulsion, altitude=0.0, eta_total=None) -> float      # [meters]
  • Endurance: E_battery × η / P_R_min (at best endurance speed)
  • Range: E_battery × η × (L/D)_max / W (at best range speed)

If eta_total is None, overall efficiency is estimated from the motor and propeller models.

docs.title: Envelope
docs.text:
Module: aerisplane.mission.envelope

compute_envelope(aircraft, weight_result, CL_max=1.4, altitudes=None,
                 aero_method="vlm") -> EnvelopeResult

Sweeps performance across altitudes (default: 0–3000 m in 200 m steps). Fits the drag polar once at sea level and evaluates all performance metrics at each altitude.

docs.title: EnvelopeResult dataclass

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
weights.analyze() → WeightResult (mass, CG)
       ↓
mission.performance() → DragPolar (3 aero calls)
       ↓
mission.envelope() → EnvelopeResult (no extra aero calls)
mission.analyze() → MissionResult (no extra aero calls)

The user must run weights.analyze() first. The drag polar is fitted once (3 VLM evaluations), then all subsequent performance calculations are purely analytic — no additional aero calls needed.

docs.title: aerisplane.mission.analyze(aircraft, weight_result, mission, aero_method='vlm', **aero_kwargs)
docs.text:
Run segment-by-segment mission energy budget analysis.

Fits a drag polar once at sea level, then evaluates each segment in order for duration, distance, and electrical energy required. Compares total energy to the battery capacity.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default aircraft Aircraft

Aircraft with a propulsion attribute (used for battery energy and efficiency estimation).

required weight_result WeightResult

Weight analysis result (provides total mass for power-required calculation).

required mission Mission

Mission profile with ordered segments and start altitude.

required aero_method str

Aero solver used for drag polar fitting. Default "vlm".

'vlm' **aero_kwargs

Additional keyword arguments passed to aero.analyze().

{}

Returns:

Type Description MissionResult

Total energy [J], time [s], distance [m], battery margin, feasibility flag, and per-segment breakdown.

Examples:

>>> from aerisplane.mission import analyze
>>> from aerisplane.mission.segments import Mission, Climb, Cruise, Loiter
>>> mission = Mission(
...     start_altitude=0.0,
...     segments=[
...         Climb(name="climb", velocity=14.0, climb_rate=2.0, to_altitude=100.0),
...         Cruise(name="cruise", velocity=18.0, altitude=100.0, distance=5000.0),
...         Loiter(name="loiter", velocity=16.0, altitude=100.0, duration=600.0),
...     ]
... )
>>> result = analyze(aircraft, weight_result, mission)
>>> print(f"Feasible: {result.feasible}  margin: {result.energy_margin:.1%}")

docs.title: aerisplane.mission.result.MissionResult dataclass
docs.text: Complete mission energy budget result.

docs.title: plot()
docs.text: Mission profile: energy budget bars + altitude profile.

docs.title: Propulsion — aerisplane.propulsion
docs.text: Computes the motor/propeller/battery operating point at a given throttle setting and flight velocity. Returns thrust, current draw, RPM, efficiency, C-rate, and estimated battery endurance.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
import aerisplane as ap
from aerisplane.catalog.motors import sunnysky_x2216_1250
from aerisplane.catalog.batteries import tattu_4s_5200
from aerisplane.catalog.propellers import apc_10x4_7sf
from aerisplane.core.propulsion import ESC, PropulsionSystem
from aerisplane.propulsion import analyze

propulsion = PropulsionSystem(
    motor=sunnysky_x2216_1250,
    propeller=apc_10x4_7sf,
    battery=tattu_4s_5200,
    esc=ESC(name="generic_60A", max_current=60.0, mass=0.025),
)
aircraft = ap.Aircraft(name="MyPlane", wings=[wing], propulsion=propulsion)
cond = ap.FlightCondition(velocity=16.0, altitude=0.0)

result = analyze(aircraft, cond, throttle=0.75)
print(result.report())

docs.title: aerisplane.propulsion.analyze(aircraft, condition, throttle=1.0)
docs.text:
Compute propulsion system operating point at a given throttle.

Solves motor RPM and current by matching torque supply and demand, then computes thrust, efficiency, battery C-rate, and estimated endurance.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default aircraft Aircraft

Aircraft with a propulsion attribute (:class:~aerisplane.core.propulsion.PropulsionSystem).

required condition FlightCondition

Operating point. Uses condition.velocity and condition.altitude.

required throttle float

Throttle setting in [0, 1]. Default 1.0 (full throttle).

1.0

Returns:

Type Description PropulsionResult

Thrust [N], current [A], RPM, motor efficiency, propulsive efficiency, electrical power [W], shaft power [W], endurance [s], C-rate, and over-current flag.

Raises:

Type Description ValueError

If aircraft.propulsion is None.

Examples:

>>> from aerisplane.propulsion import analyze
>>> result = analyze(aircraft, condition, throttle=0.6)
>>> print(result.report())

docs.title: aerisplane.propulsion.result.PropulsionResult dataclass
docs.text:
Result of a propulsion operating-point analysis.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default thrust_n float — Thrust [N] required current_a float — Battery current [A] required rpm float — Motor/propeller RPM required motor_efficiency float — Electrical-to-shaft efficiency [-] required propulsive_efficiency float — T*V/P_shaft [-] required electrical_power_w float — Total electrical power [W] required shaft_power_w float — Shaft power into propeller [W] required battery_endurance_s float — Time to battery depletion [s] required c_rate float — Instantaneous C-rate = current / capacity_ah [-] required over_current bool — True if current > min(motor.max_current, esc.max_current) required throttle float — Throttle [0-1] required velocity_ms float — Flight velocity [m/s] required

docs.title: Hardware catalog
docs.text:
See the Catalog page for available motors, batteries, and propellers. To use catalog hardware:

from aerisplane.catalog import list_motors

motors = list_motors()
motor = next(m for m in motors if "SunnySky" in m.name and m.kv == 1250)

docs.title: Stability API
docs.text: Module: aerisplane.stability

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
import aerisplane as ap
from aerisplane import weights, stability

# Build aircraft
aircraft = ap.Aircraft(name="my_uav", wings=[main_wing, htail, vtail],
                       fuselages=[fuse])

# Weight analysis first — CG is required for all stability work
weight_result = weights.analyze(aircraft)

# Basic static stability at a cruise condition
condition = ap.FlightCondition(velocity=18.0, altitude=100.0, alpha=4.0)
result = stability.analyze(aircraft, condition, weight_result)
print(result.report())
result.plot()

docs.title: Axes and sign conventions
docs.text:
AerisPlane uses the stability-axes convention (standard for fixed-wing analysis): - x forward, y right, z down - Positive roll (p) right wing down - Positive pitch (q) nose up - Positive yaw (r) nose right

All aerodynamic moment coefficients are normalised by q·S·c (pitching, Cm) or q·S·b (rolling Cl, yawing Cn), where S is the reference wing area, c is the MAC, and b is the span.

docs.title: The neutral point and static margin
docs.text:
Static stability in pitch is characterised by the slope of the pitching moment coefficient with angle of attack, measured about the centre of gravity:

dCm/dα < 0  →  statically stable

If alpha increases (e.g. a gust), a stable aircraft produces a nose-down moment that restores the original trim condition.

The neutral point (NP) is the special CG position where dCm/dα = 0 — the aircraft has neutral stability. It can be found from the relationship between the pitching moment and lift slopes:

x_NP = x_CG − (dCm/dα) / (dCL/dα) · c̄

This is the formula implemented in compute_derivatives(). Note that dCm/dα and dCL/dα are both taken about the same moment reference (the CG), which is why the aircraft copy has xyz_ref set to the CG before every aero evaluation.

The static margin (SM) is the normalised distance between the NP and the CG:

SM = (x_NP − x_CG) / c̄

Positive SM means the NP is aft of the CG — the aircraft is statically stable. A 10 % MAC static margin means the NP is 10 % of the wing chord behind the CG.

docs.title: Cm-alpha curve
docs.text:
The full Cm(α) relationship determines whether the aircraft can be trimmed (Cm = 0) at a flyable angle of attack. A well-designed aircraft has: - Negative slope (dCm/dα < 0) — stable - Positive Cm at zero lift — ensures trim occurs at a positive alpha (lifting condition)

The trim alpha is the root of Cm(α) = 0. AerisPlane finds it with Brent's method over a ±10° bracket around the linear estimate.

docs.title: Lift curve slope
docs.text: The lift curve slope dCL/dα (CL_alpha) is mainly a function of aspect ratio and sweep. For an elliptical lift distribution it approaches the Prandtl limit 2π/rad ≈ 0.110/deg. For real tapered wings of AR 5–10, expect 0.07–0.10/deg from VLM.

docs.title: Dihedral effect (Cl_beta)
docs.text:
When the aircraft sideslips (β > 0, nose left relative to flow), a negative roll moment is generated by geometric dihedral, wing sweep, and high-wing placement. This rolling restores wings-level:

dCl/dβ < 0  →  stable dihedral effect

Physical sources: geometric dihedral (dominant for low-wing aircraft), wing sweep (swept-back wings have a strong dihedral effect), and vertical position of the wing relative to the CG (high wing adds stability, low wing reduces it).

Too much dihedral effect combined with insufficient yaw damping can cause Dutch roll — a coupled oscillation between roll and yaw that is uncomfortable or hard to control. For RC aircraft, moderate Cl_beta (−0.01 to −0.05 /deg) is desirable.

docs.title: Weathercock stability (Cn_beta)
docs.text:
When the aircraft sideslips, a positive yawing moment yaws the nose back into the wind:

dCn/dβ > 0  →  directionally stable (weathercock effect)

The vertical tail is the primary contributor. Its moment arm and area set the directional stability level. Insufficient Cn_beta results in spiral divergence or directional instability.

docs.title: Tail volume coefficients
docs.text:
Tail volume coefficients are dimensionless sizing parameters that capture how much tail authority an aircraft has, independently of flight speed or wing loading.

Horizontal tail volume: 

Vh = (S_ht · l_ht) / (S_w · c̄_w)

where S_ht is the horizontal tail area, l_ht is the distance from the wing aerodynamic centre to the tail aerodynamic centre, S_w is the wing area, and c̄_w is the wing MAC.

Vh governs longitudinal trim authority and pitch stability contribution of the tail. A larger Vh moves the neutral point further aft relative to the CG, increasing static margin.

Vertical tail volume: 

Vv = (S_vt · l_vt) / (S_w · b_w)

where b_w is the wing span. Vv governs directional stability and rudder authority.

Typical values for the RC/UAV class (1–20 kg):

Parameter Typical range Notes Vh 0.35–0.60 Hand-launched UAVs trend higher (0.45–0.60) Vv 0.03–0.06 Flying wings use Vv from twin boom fins

Values below the lower bound usually indicate insufficient tail authority — the aircraft will be hard to trim or directionally weak. Values above the upper bound add unnecessary drag and weight.

docs.title: Finite-difference derivatives
docs.text:
All stability derivatives are computed by central finite differences. For a generic derivative dX/dα:

dX/dα ≈ [X(α + Δα) − X(α − Δα)] / (2·Δα)

Central differences are second-order accurate in Δα (error ∝ Δα²), versus first-order for forward differences (error ∝ Δα). For a linear solver like VLM this makes no difference — the derivative is exact regardless of step size — but for viscous or nonlinear solvers the central formula removes the leading error term and gives a better result at a given step size.

Step size selection:

A step too large violates the linearity assumption (the derivative is evaluated over a nonlinear part of the polar). A step too small amplifies numerical noise from the aero solver. For VLM (inviscid, linear), the step only needs to be large enough to avoid floating-point cancellation:

Variable Step Typical ΔX per step (VLM) α ±0.5 deg ΔCL ≈ 0.04, ΔCm ≈ 0.002 β ±1.0 deg ΔCl ≈ 0.005, ΔCn ≈ 0.005 q̂, p̂, r̂ ±0.001 ΔCm ≈ 0.001–0.005

For viscous or nonlinear solvers (NLL, AeroBuildup), the α and β steps are small enough to stay within the linear regime below stall.

docs.title: Nondimensional rates
docs.text:
Body-axis angular rates are nondimensionalised against the reference dimensions and airspeed:

p̂ = p·b / (2V)    (roll rate hat)
q̂ = q·c̄ / (2V)   (pitch rate hat)
r̂ = r·b / (2V)    (yaw rate hat)

These nondimensional rates are the natural arguments for aerodynamic moment derivatives because they represent the ratio of rotational tip speed to flight speed — the aerodynamically relevant quantity.

docs.title: Short-period mode
docs.text:
The short-period mode is a fast oscillation in angle of attack and pitch rate at roughly constant airspeed. Its frequency and damping depend on the pitch stiffness (Cm_alpha) and pitch damping (Cm_q):

ω_sp² = (q·S·c̄ / I_yy) · [CL_α·|Cm_q| − |Cm_α|·(CL_α + CL_q)]

ζ_sp  = (−Cm_q) · (q·S·c̄²) / (2·I_yy·V·ω_sp)

where I_yy is the pitch moment of inertia about the CG and all angle derivatives are in 1/rad (CL_α = CL_alpha × 180/π).

The formula requires both Cm_alpha < 0 (pitch stable) and Cm_q < 0 (pitch damping present) for a positive discriminant. If either condition is violated, the short-period frequency is returned as NaN.

Design targets for the short-period:

Parameter Minimum Typical target Notes ω_sp > 0 2–10 rad/s Too slow → sluggish in pitch ζ_sp > 0.35 0.5–1.0 Below 0.35 → noticeable oscillation after disturbance

docs.title: Phugoid mode
docs.text:
The phugoid is a slow exchange between kinetic and potential energy at roughly constant angle of attack. The Lanchester approximation gives:

ω_ph = g·√2 / V

ζ_ph = (CD / CL) / √2

The phugoid frequency depends only on speed — slower aircraft phugoid more slowly. Damping depends on the drag-to-lift ratio: cleaner aircraft (lower CD/CL) have less phugoid damping and oscillate longer.

For RC/UAV class aircraft flying at 10–20 m/s, the phugoid period is typically 5–15 s. Phugoid damping of 0.01–0.05 is normal — the mode is usually lightly damped but stable and slow enough to correct manually.

docs.title: Yaw-roll coupling
docs.text:
Cn_p (adverse yaw) and Cl_r (roll due to yaw) are coupling derivatives that appear in the Dutch roll mode. For a basic analysis:

  • Adverse yaw (Cn_p < 0): differential drag when rolling causes a yawing moment opposing the turn. Differential aileron deflection worsens it; Frise ailerons or rudder mixing reduces it.
  • Proverse yaw (Cn_p > 0): swept wings can produce proverse yaw (unusual on RC aircraft).
  • Roll due to yaw (Cl_r > 0): when the aircraft yaws right, the left wing (advancing) produces more lift, rolling the aircraft right into the turn — a destabilising coupling in Dutch roll.

docs.title: Trim
docs.text:
Trim is the condition where the net aerodynamic moment about the CG is zero and lift equals weight in steady level flight. Two equations must be satisfied simultaneously:

Cm(α_trim, δ_e_trim) = 0        (moment balance)
CL(α_trim, δ_e_trim) = W / (q·S)  (lift equals weight)

For a conventional tailed aircraft this is solved sequentially: 1. Find α_trim where Cm = 0 with δ_e = 0 (or at the baseline deflection). 2. Find δ_e_trim where Cm = 0 at α_trim while also satisfying the lift condition.

AerisPlane implements step 1 as a Brent root-find over a ±10° alpha bracket, then step 2 as a Brent root-find over ±25° elevator deflection. If either bracket does not contain a sign change, a warning is raised and the linear estimate (step 1) or NaN (step 2) is returned.

Trim alpha depends on: CG position, wing camber, tail incidence, and airspeed (through the required CL = W/qS — lower speed requires higher alpha).

Trim elevator depends on: CG position (more aft CG requires more up elevator to maintain Cm = 0), tail arm, and elevator effectiveness (CL_delta_e).

docs.title: CG envelope
docs.text:
The CG envelope defines the range of CG positions over which the aircraft is safely trimmable and controllable. AerisPlane computes it from the neutral point with a fixed 5 % MAC safety margin:

CG_aft_limit  = x_NP − 5 % MAC        (minimum stability margin)
CG_fwd_limit  = x_NP − SM − 5 % MAC   (elevator authority limit, minimum 5 % from LE)

The forward limit reflects the elevator's ability to hold the nose up at low speed (high alpha, high lift required). Moving the CG too far forward increases the download required from the tail, which the elevator may not be able to provide, especially near stall.

The aft limit reflects the minimum acceptable static margin. Flying with the CG at or beyond the neutral point means any pitch disturbance grows rather than damps.

Typical RC/UAV CG operating range: 15–35 % MAC. Performance-oriented aircraft (sailplanes, racing UAVs) fly at 25–35 % MAC for low trim drag; docile trainers at 15–25 % MAC for more stability margin.

docs.title: analyze()
docs.text:
aerisplane.stability.analyze(
    aircraft,
    condition,
    weight_result,
    aero_method="vlm",
    compute_rate_derivatives=False,
    **aero_kwargs,
) -> StabilityResult

Runs a full static (and optionally dynamic) stability analysis.

Steps performed internally: 1. Compute stability derivatives via central finite differences (5 aero evaluations). 2. Find trim alpha and trim elevator using Brent's method. 3. Compute horizontal and vertical tail volume coefficients. 4. Determine CG envelope limits from the neutral point. 5. Estimate dynamic stability modes (short-period, phugoid) if rate derivatives are requested.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description aircraft Aircraft — Aircraft geometry definition condition FlightCondition — Operating point (speed, altitude, alpha, beta) weight_result WeightResult — Provides CG position and total mass aero_method str "vlm" Aero solver — passed to aero.analyze() compute_rate_derivatives bool False Also compute p, q, r rate derivatives and dynamic modes (6 extra aero calls) **aero_kwargs Extra keyword arguments forwarded to aero.analyze()

docs.title: Number of aero evaluations
docs.text: Mode Evaluations What is computed Default (static only) 5 CL_alpha, Cm_alpha, Cl_beta, Cn_beta; trim; NP; CG envelope compute_rate_derivatives=True 11 All of the above plus CL_q, Cm_q, Cl_p, Cn_p, CY_p, Cn_r, Cl_r, CY_r and dynamic modes

docs.title: StabilityResult
docs.text: Returned by analyze(). All quantities are in SI units unless otherwise noted.

docs.title: Longitudinal static stability
docs.text: Field Unit Description static_margin fraction of MAC NP position ahead of CG. Positive = statically stable. Typical RC target: 5–15 % MAC. neutral_point m Neutral point x-position from the aircraft nose Cm_alpha 1/deg Pitching moment derivative. Negative = statically stable. CL_alpha 1/deg Lift curve slope (typically 0.07–0.12 /deg for conventional wings) CL_baseline — Lift coefficient at the analysis condition Cm_baseline — Pitching moment coefficient at the analysis condition

docs.title: Lateral-directional static stability
docs.text: Field Unit Description Cl_beta 1/deg Roll due to sideslip (dihedral effect). Negative = stable — aircraft rolls into the wind when sideslipped. Cn_beta 1/deg Yaw due to sideslip (weathercock stability). Positive = stable — aircraft yaws into the wind when sideslipped.

docs.title: Rate derivatives
docs.text:
These fields are None when compute_rate_derivatives=False.

All rate derivatives are per unit nondimensional rate (e.g. q̂ = qc/2V, p̂ = pb/2V, r̂ = rb/2V).

Field Nondim. rate Description CL_q qc/2V Lift due to pitch rate Cm_q qc/2V Pitch damping — negative = damped short-period Cl_p pb/2V Roll damping — negative = damped roll response Cn_p pb/2V Adverse yaw due to roll rate — negative = adverse yaw CY_p pb/2V Side force due to roll rate Cn_r rb/2V Yaw damping — negative = damped yaw response Cl_r rb/2V Roll due to yaw rate CY_r rb/2V Side force due to yaw rate

docs.title: Dynamic stability modes
docs.text:
These fields are None when rate derivatives are not computed, and NaN when the computation is numerically indeterminate (e.g. Cm_alpha and Cm_q have the wrong sign combination for the short-period formula).

Field Unit Description sp_frequency rad/s Short-period natural frequency sp_damping — Short-period damping ratio (ζ). Positive = oscillation decays. ph_frequency rad/s Phugoid natural frequency (Lanchester approximation: g√2/V) ph_damping — Phugoid damping ratio (Lanchester: CD/CL/√2). Typically low (0.01–0.05).

docs.title: Trim
docs.text: Field Unit Description trim_alpha deg Angle of attack where Cm = 0 at the current CG trim_elevator deg Elevator deflection for steady level flight. NaN if no elevator is found or elevator lacks authority (Cm does not cross zero in ±25°).

docs.title: Tail volume coefficients
docs.text:
Tail volume coefficients are standard sizing parameters for preliminary design. Typical RC/UAV values: Vh ≈ 0.35–0.60, Vv ≈ 0.03–0.06.

Field Description Vh Horizontal tail volume: S_ht · l_ht / (S_w · c̄_w). NaN if no horizontal tail found. Vv Vertical tail volume: S_vt · l_vt / (S_w · b_w). NaN if no vertical tail found.

Tail identification uses wing geometry (symmetric → horizontal, non-symmetric → vertical) and name hints ("vtail", "vertical").

docs.title: CG envelope
docs.text:
Field Description cg_forward_limit Forward CG limit as fraction of MAC from MAC leading edge cg_aft_limit Aft CG limit as fraction of MAC from MAC leading edge

Both limits include a 5 % MAC safety margin from the neutral point. The aft limit is NP − 5 % MAC; the forward limit is NP − SM − 5 % MAC (minimum 5 % from LE).

docs.title: Reference values
docs.text: Field Unit Description cg_x m CG x-position used for this analysis mac m Mean aerodynamic chord of the main wing mac_le_x m MAC leading edge x-position

docs.title: report()
docs.text:
result.report() -> str

Returns a formatted plain-text stability report. Print it with print(result.report()).

Sections always included: longitudinal static stability, lateral-directional static stability, trim, tail volume coefficients, CG envelope, and baseline coefficients.

Sections shown only when rate derivatives are available: rate derivatives table, dynamic stability modes (short-period and phugoid frequencies, periods, and damping ratios).

docs.title: plot()
docs.text:
result.plot() -> matplotlib.figure.Figure

Returns a 1×2 figure (14×6 inches):

  • Left panel — Pitch Stability: Cm vs alpha. If an alpha sweep was stored (e.g. from a polar run), the full curve is plotted. Otherwise a tangent line ±3° around the trim alpha is shown. Trim alpha is marked with a vertical dotted line.
  • Right panel — CG Envelope: A MAC bar (LE at 0, TE at 1) with the allowable CG range shaded, the current CG marked with a triangle, and the neutral point with a diamond. Forward and aft CG limits are drawn as dashed red lines.

docs.title: compute_derivatives()
docs.text:
Lower-level function used internally by analyze(). Call this directly when you only need the raw finite-difference derivatives without trim or tail volume computation.

from aerisplane.stability.derivatives import compute_derivatives

deriv = compute_derivatives(
    aircraft,
    condition,
    weight_result,
    aero_method="vlm",
    compute_rate_derivatives=False,
    **aero_kwargs,
) -> DerivativeResult

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Same as analyze(). The aircraft's moment reference is overridden internally to the CG from weight_result.

docs.title: Finite-difference step sizes
docs.text: Perturbation Step Rationale α (angle of attack) ±0.5 deg Small enough for linearity, large enough to avoid VLM noise β (sideslip) ±1.0 deg Larger step because lateral derivatives are smaller p̂, q̂, r̂ (nondimensional rate) ±0.001 Standard nondimensional rate perturbation

docs.title: DerivativeResult fields
docs.text: Field Unit Description CL_alpha 1/deg dCL/dα Cm_alpha 1/deg dCm/dα Cl_beta 1/deg dCl/dβ Cn_beta 1/deg dCn/dβ neutral_point m Neutral point x-position static_margin fraction of MAC (NP − CG) / MAC cg_x m CG x-position used mac m Reference chord mac_le_x m MAC leading edge x-position baseline AeroResult Full aero result at the analysis condition CL_q, Cm_q per qc/2V Pitch rate derivatives (None if not computed) Cl_p, Cn_p, CY_p per pb/2V Roll rate derivatives (None if not computed) Cn_r, Cl_r, CY_r per rb/2V Yaw rate derivatives (None if not computed)

docs.title: Sign conventions
docs.text: Sign Physical meaning Cm_alpha < 0 Statically stable — nose pitches down when alpha increases Cl_beta < 0 Dihedral effect — roll restores wings level when sideslipped Cn_beta > 0 Weathercock — yaws into the wind when sideslipped Cm_q < 0 Pitch rate damps short-period oscillation Cl_p < 0 Roll damping — roll rate decays without input Cn_r < 0 Yaw damping — yaw rate decays without input

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
weights.analyze() → WeightResult (CG, mass, inertia)
       ↓
stability.analyze() → StabilityResult
       ↑ uses aero.analyze() internally (5 or 11 calls)

analyze() requires a WeightResult — always run weights.analyze() first.

docs.title: Stability check at cruise
docs.text:
import aerisplane as ap
from aerisplane import weights, stability

weight_result = weights.analyze(aircraft)
condition = ap.FlightCondition(velocity=18.0, altitude=100.0, alpha=3.0)

result = stability.analyze(aircraft, condition, weight_result)
print(result.report())

# Check stability margins
print(f"Static margin  : {result.static_margin * 100:.1f} % MAC")
print(f"Cm_alpha       : {result.Cm_alpha:+.5f} /deg")
print(f"Cn_beta (wc)   : {result.Cn_beta:+.5f} /deg")
print(f"Trim alpha     : {result.trim_alpha:.1f} deg")

docs.title: Full analysis with dynamic modes
docs.text:
result = stability.analyze(
    aircraft, condition, weight_result,
    compute_rate_derivatives=True,
)

# Short-period period and damping
import numpy as np
if result.sp_frequency and not np.isnan(result.sp_frequency):
    sp_period = 2 * np.pi / result.sp_frequency
    print(f"Short-period: T={sp_period:.2f} s, ζ={result.sp_damping:.3f}")

# Phugoid period
if result.ph_frequency and not np.isnan(result.ph_frequency):
    ph_period = 2 * np.pi / result.ph_frequency
    print(f"Phugoid: T={ph_period:.1f} s, ζ={result.ph_damping:.4f}")

docs.title: CG sensitivity sweep
docs.text:
Evaluate how static margin changes as CG moves fore and aft (e.g. during a payload trade).

import numpy as np
from aerisplane.weights.result import ComponentOverride

cg_offsets = np.linspace(-0.05, 0.05, 9)  # ±50 mm from nominal
margins = []

for dx in cg_offsets:
    # Shift payload CG by dx to move total CG
    result_w = weights.analyze(aircraft, overrides={
        "payload": ComponentOverride(
            mass=0.200,
            cg=np.array([nominal_cg_x + dx, 0.0, 0.0]),
        )
    })
    result_s = stability.analyze(aircraft, condition, result_w)
    margins.append(result_s.static_margin * 100)

for dx, sm in zip(cg_offsets * 1000, margins):
    print(f"CG offset {dx:+.0f} mm → static margin {sm:.1f} % MAC")

docs.title: Sweep alpha to capture the Cm(α) curve
docs.text:
The trim solver uses a single linear estimate. For a full Cm-vs-alpha sweep, call compute_derivatives() directly and interrogate the baseline result at each alpha.

from aerisplane.stability.derivatives import compute_derivatives
import numpy as np

alphas = np.linspace(-4, 14, 10)
Cms = []
for a in alphas:
    cond = ap.FlightCondition(velocity=18.0, altitude=100.0, alpha=a)
    deriv = compute_derivatives(aircraft, cond, weight_result)
    Cms.append(deriv.baseline.Cm)

# Find trim alpha by interpolation
trim_a = np.interp(0.0, Cms[::-1], alphas[::-1])
print(f"Trim alpha (from sweep) = {trim_a:.2f} deg")

docs.title: Checking tail sizing
docs.text:
result = stability.analyze(aircraft, condition, weight_result)

# Rule-of-thumb targets for RC/UAV class
Vh_target = (0.35, 0.60)
Vv_target = (0.03, 0.06)

print(f"Vh = {result.Vh:.3f}  (target {Vh_target[0]:.2f}–{Vh_target[1]:.2f})")
print(f"Vv = {result.Vv:.3f}  (target {Vv_target[0]:.3f}–{Vv_target[1]:.3f})")

docs.title: aerisplane.stability.analyze(aircraft, condition, weight_result, aero_method='vlm', compute_rate_derivatives=False, **aero_kwargs)
docs.text:
Run a full static stability analysis.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default aircraft Aircraft

Aircraft geometry definition.

required condition FlightCondition

Operating point (velocity, altitude, alpha, beta).

required weight_result WeightResult

Weight analysis result providing CG position.

required aero_method str

Aero solver method (default "vlm").

'vlm' compute_rate_derivatives bool

If True, also compute p, q, r rate derivatives and dynamic stability modes (short-period, phugoid). Adds 6 extra aero evaluations. Default False.

False **aero_kwargs

Additional keyword arguments passed to aero.analyze().

{}

Returns:

Type Description StabilityResult

Complete stability analysis result.

docs.title: aerisplane.stability.result.StabilityResult dataclass
docs.text:
Static and dynamic stability analysis result.

Attributes:

Name Type Description static_margin float

Static margin as fraction of MAC (positive = stable).

neutral_point float

Neutral point x-position [m] from aircraft origin.

Cm_alpha float

Pitch moment derivative dCm/dalpha [1/deg]. Negative = stable.

CL_alpha float

Lift curve slope dCL/dalpha [1/deg].

Cl_beta float

Roll-due-to-sideslip dCl/dbeta [1/deg]. Negative = stable (dihedral effect).

Cn_beta float

Yaw-due-to-sideslip dCn/dbeta [1/deg]. Positive = stable (weathercock).

CL_q float or None

Lift due to pitch rate (q_hat = qc/2V). None if rate derivatives not computed.

Cm_q float or None

Pitch damping derivative (negative = damped). None if rate derivatives not computed.

Cl_p float or None

Roll damping derivative (negative = damped). None if rate derivatives not computed.

Cn_p float or None

Yaw due to roll rate — adverse yaw. None if rate derivatives not computed.

CY_p float or None

Side force due to roll rate. None if rate derivatives not computed.

Cn_r float or None

Yaw damping derivative (negative = damped). None if rate derivatives not computed.

Cl_r float or None

Roll due to yaw rate. None if rate derivatives not computed.

CY_r float or None

Side force due to yaw rate. None if rate derivatives not computed.

sp_frequency float or None

Short-period natural frequency [rad/s].

sp_damping float or None

Short-period damping ratio (positive = damped).

ph_frequency float or None

Phugoid natural frequency [rad/s] (Lanchester approximation).

ph_damping float or None

Phugoid damping ratio (Lanchester: CD/CL/sqrt(2)).

trim_alpha float

Angle of attack for Cm = 0 at current CG [deg].

trim_elevator float

Elevator deflection for trimmed level flight [deg]. NaN if no elevator found or insufficient authority.

Vh float

Horizontal tail volume coefficient. NaN if no htail found.

Vv float

Vertical tail volume coefficient. NaN if no vtail found.

cg_forward_limit float

Forward CG limit as fraction of MAC from wing LE.

cg_aft_limit float

Aft CG limit as fraction of MAC from wing LE.

cg_x float

CG x-position used for analysis [m].

mac float

Mean aerodynamic chord [m].

mac_le_x float

MAC leading edge x-position [m].

CL_baseline float

Lift coefficient at the analysis condition.

Cm_baseline float

Pitching moment coefficient at the analysis condition.

docs.title: report()
docs.text:
Formatted plain-text stability analysis report.

Sections always included: • Longitudinal Static Stability — CL_alpha, Cm_alpha, neutral point, CG position, static margin, and a STABLE / UNSTABLE verdict. • Lateral-Directional Static Stability — Cl_beta (dihedral effect) and Cn_beta (weathercock), each with an OK / WARN flag. • Trim — trim alpha and trim elevator (or N/A if unavailable). • Tail Volume Coefficients — Vh and Vv (or N/A if not identified). • CG Envelope — forward and aft limits as % MAC, with current CG. • Baseline Coefficients — CL and Cm at the analysis condition.

Sections shown only when rate derivatives were computed: • Rate Derivatives — CL_q, Cm_q (pitch damping), Cl_p, Cn_p (roll damping, adverse yaw), Cn_r, Cl_r (yaw damping). • Dynamic Stability Modes — short-period ω, T, ζ and phugoid ω, T, ζ (Lanchester approximation); N/A printed when computation failed.

Returns:

Type Description str

Multi-line string suitable for printing to a terminal or saving to a text file. No trailing newline.

docs.title: plot()
docs.text:
Static stability summary figure (two panels).

Left panel — Pitch Stability (Cm vs alpha): If a Cm-alpha sweep was stored in _alpha_sweep / _Cm_sweep (e.g. from a polar run), the full curve is plotted. Otherwise a tangent line spanning ±3° around trim_alpha is drawn, labelled with Cm_alpha. A dashed horizontal line marks Cm = 0, and a dotted vertical line marks the trim angle of attack.

Right panel — CG Envelope: A horizontal bar representing the full MAC (leading edge at 0, trailing edge at 1) is drawn in light grey. The allowable CG range (forward_limit to aft_limit, both as fractions of MAC) is shaded. The current CG is marked with a downward triangle and the neutral point with a diamond. Dashed and dash-dot red lines show the forward and aft CG limits. LE / TE labels appear below the bar. The figure title states the static margin in % MAC.

Returns:

Type Description Figure

1×2 figure, 14×6 inches. Call plt.show() or fig.savefig(...) on the returned object.

docs.title: Structures — aerisplane.structures
docs.text: Wing spar structural analysis using an Euler–Bernoulli beam model. Checks bending yield, shear yield, buckling, and tip deflection against ultimate design loads. Requires Spar definitions on wing cross-sections.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
import aerisplane as ap
from aerisplane.aero import analyze as aero_analyze
from aerisplane.weights import analyze as weight_analyze
from aerisplane.structures import analyze
from aerisplane.core.structures import Spar, TubeSection
from aerisplane.catalog.materials import CFRP_UD

# Add a spar to the wing root and tip cross-sections
spar = Spar(
    position=0.25,
    tube=TubeSection(outer_diameter=0.018, wall_thickness=0.0015),
    material=CFRP_UD,
)
wing.xsecs[0].spar = spar
wing.xsecs[1].spar = spar

cond = ap.FlightCondition(velocity=20.0, altitude=0.0, alpha=6.0)
aero_r = aero_analyze(aircraft, cond, method="vlm")
weight_r = weight_analyze(aircraft)
result = analyze(aircraft, aero_r, weight_r, n_limit=4.0)
result.report()

docs.title: aerisplane.structures.analyze(aircraft, aero_result, weight_result, n_limit=3.5, safety_factor=1.5, gust_velocity=9.0, stability_result=None, n_stations=50)

3.5 safety_factor float

Safety factor applied to limit load factor (default 1.5 -> ultimate).

9.0 stability_result StabilityResult or None

If provided, CL_alpha from stability analysis is used for gust n.

None n_stations int

Number of spanwise beam integration stations per wing (default 50).

50
